text: The surgeon visualizes the blind loop, afferent loop, and anastomotic defect. A piece of gauze is inserted into the afferent loop to prevent accidental closure during subsequent transanal suturing.
The surgeon inspects a clean granulating cavity, noting increased fibrosis due to multiple endosponge changes. Mobilization of the rectal cuff from surrounding structures is required.
The surgeon places a six to eight French suction drain into the old abscess cavity before closing the defect, aiming to prevent the formation of a new abscess.

The surgeon inserts a suction drain one to two centimeters distal to the anastomotic defect, positioning it immediately posterior to the rectal wall. The drain is then secured using a fast-resorbable Ficryl-III-Zero suture.

The transanal platform is removed and repositioned to keep the drain outside its lumen, minimizing interference during suture placement. The drain is visibly fixated outside the lumen of the transanal platform.

The surgeon closes the cavity using separate interrupted full thickness Ficryl-II-Zero sutures. Gas is visible in the afferent loop, preventing its complete closure during the suturing process. After the defect is fully closed, the gas will be removed to ensure the afferent loop remains patent.
